Extensive experiments on a real-world Dutch job-posting dataset of over 1 million records demonstrate that GAT-MDN significantly outperforms a non-graph MLP-MDN baseline in both Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) and Mean Squared Error (MSE).

</details>


### [462] ["That's AI Slop, You Bot!" Studying Accusations, Evidence, and Credibility in Online Discourse Towards LLM-Generated Comments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12073)
*Jason Miklian,John E. Katsos*

Main category: cs.SI

TL;DR: This paper explores readers' reactions to AI-generated text by analyzing accusations of AI use in online comments, finding that such accusations often serve as social gatekeeping rather than accurate identification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to understand how readers respond to AI-generated text, especially in terms of accusations of AI use, and to explore what this reveals about societal attitudes toward generative AI.

Method: The study analyzed 25 million online comments, used LLMs to assess accusations, and conducted sentiment analysis, speech-act coding, and control tests to compare accused versus non-accused comments.

Result: Accusations of AI use increased significantly, with a growing trend to label suspicious writing as "AI slop." However, these accusations did not consistently align with statistical markers of AI-generated prose; instead, they functioned as a form of social gatekeeping.

Conclusion: Reader-side social dynamics around authenticity accusations play a key role in shaping reactions to AI writing, emphasizing in-group signaling over accurate detection, a challenge that detection technologies cannot fully address.

Abstract: Generative AI has made fluent prose cheap to produce, breaking the old promise to readers that good writing meant real thinking. How have readers responded, and what can this tell us about changing anti-AI attitudes? We analyzed 25 million comments from Hacker News and Reddit (2023-2026), combining LLM judgment on 7,500 sampled accusations of AI use, sentiment trajectories, speech-act coding of 300 confirmed accusations of AI use, and a matched-control test of accused versus non-accused parent comments. We found that the pejorative-label share of accusations rose more than tenfold on both platforms while a placebo vocabulary of pre-2022 inauthenticity terms (shill, astroturf) did not. This shift reflected a fast-growing trend of branding any suspicious or seemingly inauthentic prose as "AI slop". The slop frame now constitutes 94 percent of pejorative mentions, with the dominant comments shifting in tone from mockery toward gatekeeping and structural protest. The key surprise comes from a matched-control test which found that prose features that statistically distinguish AI from human text do not predict which human text gets accused as AI. The new accusations work as social gatekeeping of perceived authenticity without actually screening for AI. This research extends signaling theory by showing that substitute signals used socially can grow even when inaccurate if the underlying detection problem cannot be solved at the non-expert level. It shows that AI's effects on writing from the reader side are distinct from those on the production (writer) side. Detection technology cannot resolve this dynamic because the social function of accusations is increasingly to perform social gatekeeping and in-group signaling as opposed to identifying AI-generated writing.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.dis-nn'></div>

# cond-mat.dis-nn [[Back]](#toc)

### [463] [Exact distribution of the output of a deep-layered machine](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11965)
*Thomas M. A. Fink*

Main category: cond-mat.dis-nn

TL;DR: The paper studies the statistical behavior of output functions in deep-layered Boolean machines, highlighting biases influenced by depth and width.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the statistics driving the global output function in deep-layered machines.

Method: An exact derivation of finite-depth output distribution based on the machine's width (k) and depth (n).

Result: Finds a bias in favor of functions with extreme Hamming weights at higher depths, peaking dependent on the width before collapsing to constant outputs.

Conclusion: Output behaviors in deep-layered machines exhibit structured biases, with implications for understanding deep learning and Boolean computation mechanics.

Abstract: Deep-layered machines, in which each node computes a Boolean function of all nodes below it, underpin deep learning and digital computation. Yet the statistics of their global output function remain poorly understood. We derive the exact finite-depth distribution of the output of a machine with width $k$ and depth $n$. The distribution depends only on the Hamming weight of the output, and as $n$ increases favors functions with low and high Hamming weights. But this bias peaks at a crossover depth proportional to $2^k$ before collapsing onto the constant functions true and false.

</details>


<div id='quant-ph'></div>

# quant-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [464] [Tensor-Network-Based Distributed Quantum Dynamics on Independent Quantum Computers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11579)
*Anurag Dwivedi,Melissa C. Revelle,Daniel S. Lobser,Brian K. McFarland,Edward C. Tortorici,Christopher G. Yale,Susan M. Clark,Philip Richerme,Srinivasan S. Iyengar*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper presents a tensor network-based approach for distributed quantum computing to simulate chemical wavepacket dynamics, enhancing efficiency through decomposition into independent tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address computational challenges in simulating chemical wavepacket dynamics and achieve accurate vibrational spectra of complex molecular systems.

Method: A tensor-network representation is used to transform entangled quantum evolution into parallel computational tasks suitable for distributed quantum/classical computation. The method is validated using Sandia National Laboratories' trapped-ion quantum computer with optimized gate designs.

Result: The approach achieves spectroscopic accuracy (agreement within 4 cm^-1) for vibrational spectra of protonated water clusters, surpassing experimental and traditional computational challenges.

Conclusion: The method proves effective for simulating complex molecular systems using distributed quantum computing and highlights the feasibility of hybrid quantum/classical approaches.

Abstract: We present an approach based on tensor networks for distributed quantum computing simulation of chemical wavepacket dynamics in a continuous variable representation. The central idea is that the tensor-network representation of the multidimensional time-evolution operator naturally induces an elevated Hilbert space where the dynamics decomposes into a set of independent lower-dimensional propagations. This transformation converts an entangled quantum evolution into a set of parallel computational tasks that can be executed asynchronously across heterogeneous quantum and classical computing architectures. The resulting formalism establishes a direct connection between tensor-network decompositions, uniformly controlled quantum circuits, and asynchronous distributed quantum computing. The approach is developed with a goal towards hybrid quantum/classical implementation, and is appropriate for a general heterogeneous mixture of quantum hardware systems. The experimental realization of the asynchronously distributed quantum processes that arise from the tensor-network decomposition are carried out on the Sandia National Laboratories' trapped-ion quantum computer, where the circuits are compiled using native partial-entangling $XX(θ)$ gates, reducing the expected two-qubit gate infidelity by more than 30\% relative to conventional fully entangling decompositions. We demonstrate the methodology by quantum computing the vibrational spectra of a small protonated water cluster that shows critical quantum nuclear behavior. Such water cluster systems have been found to be challenging for experimental action spectroscopy and for theory, and here, for the first time, we provide results for vibrational spectroscopy that are in agreement with the respective classical results to within 4cm$^{-1}$, thus allowing for the potential for spectroscopic accuracy from quantum computations.

</details>


### [465] [Sparsified Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks for Interpretable Quantum State Tomography](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11814)
*Xinge Wu,Huaxin Wang,Jiajun Liu,Ruiqing He,Jiandong Shang,Hengliang Guo,Qiang Chen*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper investigates if sparsified Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs) can provide interpretable quantum state reconstruction while maintaining consistency with known physical structures, using a GHZ-family benchmark.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To explore if machine-learning models, such as sparsified Kolmogorov-Arnold Networks (KANs), can serve not only as regression tools for quantum state tomography but also offer interpretable physical insights aligned with known Pauli structures.

Method: The study employs a three-qubit GHZ-family benchmark to reconstruct three key variables (population imbalance, real off-diagonal component, imaginary off-diagonal component) using KAN. Ablation studies and robustness tests under varying noise and shot counts were carried out to determine the model's interpretability and structure consistency.

Result: KAN successfully identifies relevant Pauli structures and organizes measurements in line with the GHZ analytic grouping. The internal pathways of the neural network exhibit structural interpretability, and the sparse formula recovery aligns with canonical Pauli relations.

Conclusion: The KAN approach facilitates structural interpretability at the pathway level within quantum state reconstruction models, ensuring alignment with known physical structures and providing a tool for auditing learned rules against theoretical benchmarks.

Abstract: Machine-learning approaches to quantum state tomography can achieve high reconstruction fidelity, but the physical structure used by the trained model often remains implicit. Here we ask whether a sparsified Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) can be used not only as a regressor, but also as an inspectable reconstruction rule whose internal organization can be checked against known Pauli structure. We study a controlled three-qubit GHZ-family benchmark in which all 63 non-identity Pauli expectation values are used to reconstruct three GHZ-subspace variables: the population imbalance $z$, the real off-diagonal component $c$, and the imaginary off-diagonal component $s$. Under finite-shot sampling and depolarizing noise, external ablation identifies the extended 12-channel GHZ-relevant Pauli set from the 63 measurements, with exact top-12 recovery across the tested shot counts and depolarizing-noise strengths. These support patterns remain stable across multi-seed random-initialization and noise-level analyses, and collapse under random-label controls. The dominant pruned input-hidden-output pathways organize Z-type population observables and X/Y off-diagonal observables in a pattern consistent with the analytic GHZ Pauli grouping, and sparse formula recovery recovers the canonical signed Pauli relations. The contribution of the KAN is therefore pathway-level structural interpretability within a neural reconstruction model, rather than superior sparse regression. Together with negative controls, these probes provide a consistency chain for auditing learned reconstruction rules against known physical structure.

</details>


### [466] [Family-Aware Residual Architecture for Predicting Quantum Circuit Simulation Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11620)
*Honjar Xing,Yehong Jiang,Xianbang Wang,Zehua Wang,Zhicheng Jiang*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: This paper introduces a neural architecture that predicts simulation parameters (thresholds and runtimes) for approximate tensor-network quantum circuit simulators, reducing costly trial-and-error processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Selecting optimal approximation parameters for approximate tensor-network simulators is time-intensive due to trial-and-error methods, especially because quantum circuits differ in simulation complexity based on their algorithm family.

Method: The authors propose a neural architecture with family-conditioned residual corrections that leverages a pretrained family classifier and domain-informed features to predict parameters like fidelity thresholds and runtimes for quantum circuit simulations.

Result: The model achieves 79.5% exact threshold accuracy (91.2% within one rung), a runtime prediction correlation of $R^2 = 0.82$, and inference time of ~50 ms, validated on circuits with 7-130 qubits and 10 algorithmic families.

Conclusion: Incorporating algorithm family information significantly enhances the accuracy of simulation cost predictions, proving it a key feature for optimizing tensor-network simulators.

Abstract: Approximate tensor-network simulators enable classical simulation of quantum circuits beyond the reach of exact methods, but selecting optimal approximation parameters -- such as bond dimension thresholds -- remains a costly trial-and-error process. We present a family-aware neural architecture that predicts both the minimum approximation threshold required to achieve target fidelity and the expected wall-clock runtime for quantum circuit simulation, given only the circuit's OpenQASM description and execution context. Our key insight is that quantum circuits from different algorithmic families (e.g., QFT, Grover, VQE) exhibit fundamentally distinct simulation cost profiles due to their differing entanglement structures. We employ family-conditioned residual corrections -- additive, family-specific adjustments atop a shared backbone, drawing on established conditional computation techniques -- enabling the model to capture both universal circuit properties and algorithmic nuances. The architecture incorporates a pretrained family classifier (97.5% accuracy) and domain-informed algorithm fingerprint features derived from gate-composition heuristics. Evaluated on circuits spanning 7--130 qubits across 10 algorithm families, our system achieves 79.5% exact threshold accuracy (91.2% within one rung) and $R^2 = 0.82$ runtime correlation, with inference completing in approximately 50 ms -- replacing trial-and-error simulation runs that may take minutes to hours. Ablation studies confirm that family-aware modeling provides the single largest performance improvement (+3.2 percentage points), validating the hypothesis that algorithm family is a first-class feature for simulation cost prediction.

</details>


### [467] [Higher-Order Token Interactions via Quantum Attention](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11673)
*Jian Xu,Chao Li,Delu Zeng,John Paisley,Qibin Zhao*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA), a quantum-based model enabling higher-order token interactions with reduced computational costs and improved efficiency compared to classical self-attention layers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: A single layer of dot-product self-attention in classical models only captures pairwise interactions, and extending this to higher-order interactions requires significant computational resources or deeper architectures. The authors seek to address these limitations by leveraging quantum computation.

Method: The authors propose QHA, a quantum attention mechanism based on data re-uploading and an all-to-all non-Clifford entangler, enabling higher-order interactions with shallow circuits. They prove its theoretical advantages in expressivity and trainability and validate these properties empirically.

Result: QHA outperforms classical self-attention in representing high-order interactions, maintaining trainability, and showing strong empirical performance in synthetic and real-world tasks, despite using significantly fewer parameters.

Conclusion: QHA is a compact and efficient mechanism for capturing high-order correlations, making it a powerful tool for tasks requiring complex interaction detection in various fields such as genetics, noisy learning problems, and graph analysis.

Abstract: Standard dot-product self-attention computes, in a single layer, only pairwise (order-2) interactions between tokens; representing a generic order-$k$ interaction is known to require either super-quadratic resources in one layer or composition across depth. We introduce \textbf{Quantum Higher-Order Attention (QHA)}, a shallow, hardware-realizable quantum attention head that, via data re-uploading and an all-to-all non-Clifford entangler, synthesizes order-$k$ token interactions inside the circuit and exposes them through a local single-qubit read-out. We prove (i) an expressivity separation: any single standard self-attention layer with embedding dimension $m$, $H$ heads and $p$-bit precision satisfying $mHp=o(N/\log\log N)$ cannot represent the order-$k$ correlation family that one QHA head represents with circuit depth $O(\log k)$ ($O(k)$ two-qubit gates); and (ii) a trainability guarantee for its local-design instantiation: with a local read-out and $O(\log n)$ depth the gradient variance is $Ω(1/\mathrm{poly}(n))$ (no barren plateau), which we confirm empirically -- while being explicit that the more expressive all-to-all instantiation we benchmark is trained empirically and shows exponentially decaying gradients. Empirically, at a $6.5\times$ smaller parameter budget, QHA generalizes hidden-subset parity of every order $k\le6$ from disjoint inputs, whereas the larger classical attention head collapses past order~2; consistent with theory, the size of the advantage tracks the target's Fourier degree - largest for parity and shrinking when low-order structure is present. As an application, QHA serves as a compact high-order interaction detector across three domains - genetic epistasis, learning-parity-with-noise, and graph triangle detection - reaching the noise ceiling at the smallest parameter budget where field-standard linear methods fail.

</details>


### [468] [Quantum Occam Learning: Sample-Supported Expressibility for Circuit-Based Quantum Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12211)
*Jeongho Bang,Kyoungho Cho,Jeongwoo Jae*

Main category: quant-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces an information-theoretic framework to relate quantum circuit expressibility with learnability when using finite samples, proposing sample-supported bounds and adaptive model selection principles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of balancing the expressibility of quantum machine learning ansatz with the finite-sample constraint in learning unknown quantum states, providing a statistically meaningful approach.

Method: The paper develops an Occam theory for quantum data based on metric entropy, introduces definitions like best $G$-gate approximation error, and provides theorems for sample efficiency, agnostic learning, and adaptive model selection for bounded circuit complexities.

Result: Key results include an agnostic quantum Occam theorem with sample complexity $theta(G/epsilon^2)$, an adaptive model-selection framework for circuit complexity, and lower bounds showing sample-supported expressibility constraints at accuracy levels.

Conclusion: The circuit complexity in quantum machine learning is reframed as an adaptive statistical resource, enabling improved model selection and grounding learnability in finite-sample scenarios through bounded circuit expressibility.

Abstract: A central principle in quantum machine learning is that an ansatz should be expressive enough to represent the quantum data of interest. Yet, the expressibility is statistically meaningful only insofar as it can be learned from finitely many copies of an unknown quantum state. In this work, we develop an information-theoretic Occam theory for quantum data generated by finite-size quantum circuits. For the class $S_{n,G}$ of $n$-qubit pure states preparable with at most $G$ two-qubit gates, a metric-entropy argument gives the realizable sample law $\widetildeΘ(G/ε^2)$ in the circuit-limited regime. For an arbitrary source $\hatρ$, we introduce the best $G$-gate approximation error $d_G(\hatρ)$ and the approximate circuit complexity $C_η(\hatρ)$. We prove an agnostic quantum Occam theorem: with $M$ copies, one can learn up to the best $G$-gate approximation error plus a statistical penalty $\widetilde{O}(\sqrt{G/M})$. We then remove the need to know $G$ in advance through an adaptive model-selection theorem whose oracle inequality selects the circuit complexity justified by the data. Matching lower bounds yield a sample-supported expressibility law: at trace-distance accuracy $ε$, $M$ samples can support only $G_{\rm supported} \simeq Mε^2$ gates, up to logarithmic factors and tomography saturation at $2^n$. Thus, the circuit complexity becomes an adaptive statistical resource rather than a static promise. Our framework turns bounded circuit complexity into a model-selection principle for quantum machine learning.

</details>


<div id='cond-mat.mtrl-sci'></div>

# cond-mat.mtrl-sci [[Back]](#toc)

### [469] [Modelling magnetic material properties with uncertainty-aware neural networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11870)
*Clemens Wager,Heisam Moustafa,Alexander Kovacs,Qais Ali,Harald Oezelt,Hayate Yamano,Masao Yano,Noritsugu Sakuma,Hyuga Hosoi,Akihito Kinoshita,Tetsuya Shoji,Akira Kato,Thomas Schrefl*

Main category: cond-mat.mtrl-sci

TL;DR: The paper explores uncertainty quantification in machine learning models for improved prediction reliability in materials research, with a focus on permanent magnets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the challenges posed by scarce high-quality data and out-of-distribution predictions in machine learning for materials discovery, particularly in permanent magnet research.

Method: The study benchmarks classical and modern machine learning models using methods like Gaussian negative log-likelihood loss and dropout-based Bayesian approximation for uncertainty quantification. It also applies these techniques to a graph neural network for predicting coercivity from microstructural data.

Result: The studies show that uncertainty quantification improves the reliability of predictions and can be effectively transferred to more complex modeling tasks.

Conclusion: Uncertainty quantification methods enhance prediction trustworthiness and generalize well across different machine learning tasks in materials science.

Abstract: Machine learning is increasingly applied to accelerate the discovery of novel materials by exploring large compositional and structural design spaces. Yet, the scarcity of high-quality data and the frequent need for out-of-distribution prediction introduce substantial uncertainty, making the assessment of model reliability essential. In this work, we investigate uncertainty quantification as a means to evaluate model confidence in the context of permanent magnet research. In a first study, we benchmark classical and modern machine learning models for predicting intrinsic magnetic properties, focusing on the quality of their uncertainty estimates. We apply Gaussian negative log-likelihood loss and dropout-based Bayesian approximation as practical strategies for estimating predictive uncertainty. In a second study, we transfer these architectural features for uncertainty estimation to a more complex task: predicting coercivity from microstructural information using a graph neural network. Together, these studies demonstrate that uncertainty quantification not only enhances the trustworthiness of predictions but is also transferable across different modeling tasks.

</details>


<div id='physics.ao-ph'></div>

# physics.ao-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [470] [An Ocean Model Ported by a Large Language Model: Experience and Lessons from FESOM2 (Fortran to C to C++/Kokkos)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11356)
*Nikolay V. Koldunov,Suvarchal K. Cheedela,Sergey Danilov,Dmitry Sidorenko,Sebastian Beyer,Thomas Jung*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: This paper discusses the use of large language models (LLMs) to translate and optimize an ocean simulation code, preserving its physics while making it GPU-compatible and improving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation arises from the need to modernize and optimize production-grade scientific code, such as geophysical models, by leveraging advanced computing architectures like GPUs while ensuring the physics and functionality of the code remain intact. LLMs are explored as tools for automating such translations.

Method: The authors utilized agentic coding LLMs under expert guidance to port the FESOM2 Fortran code to C for numerical consistency and then to C++/Kokkos for GPU performance. Their approach emphasized breaking the process into two validation-focused stages to ensure fidelity: (1) replicating numerics from Fortran to C and (2) introducing parallelism in the transition from C to Kokkos.

Result: The translated C version replicates the original Fortran results, while the Kokkos version achieves bit-for-bit accuracy on CPUs and near-consistent results on GPUs over multi-year runs. The optimized GPU implementation exhibits a speedup of 1.6–3.7 times over a CPU node, fulfilling simulation speed requirements for production workloads.

Conclusion: LLM-assisted translation combined with domain expertise and rigorous validation can accelerate the migration of complex scientific models across languages and architectures while maintaining their scientific integrity. This success demonstrates the viability of LLMs in modernizing performance-intensive legacy codes.

Abstract: Large language models (LLMs) can translate and modify source code, and have been shown to do so for codes of different complexity. Whether they can port a complete, production geophysical model to a different language without degrading its physics has not been established. We demonstrate that LLM-assisted code translation can preserve the physics of a complete production ocean model while moving it into a modern performance-portable form. We report our experience using an agentic LLM coding assistant, directed by domain experts, to port the FESOM2 unstructured mesh ocean--sea-ice model (about 74000 lines of core Fortran) first to C and then to C++/Kokkos for performance portability across CPUs and GPUs. We describe the practices that proved necessary, what worked and what did not, and the failure modes that we encountered. Three practices mattered most: translating in two stages that separate reproducing the numerics (Fortran to a clean C reference) from introducing parallelism (C to Kokkos); requiring a strictly literal translation in which the assistant was not permitted to ``improve'' the source; and validating each stage against an acceptance criterion suited to it.
  The C port reproduces the original Fortran at the level of long-term simulation statistics over five years. The Kokkos port is bit-for-bit identical to the C reference on CPU and statistically close on GPU over multi-year runs. On eddy-rich meshes up to 7.4 million surface vertices a single A100 GPU node runs 1.6--3.7 times faster than a CPU node, reaching the 1-2 simulated-years-per-day required for production integrations. The result is more than a single GPU port: by following a clear validation procedure, an LLM moved a full Fortran ocean model into another language and onto accelerators while preserving its physics in a matter of weeks.

</details>


### [471] [Urban Heat MiniCubes: An AI-Ready dataset for urban heat research](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11534)
*Jonathan Starfeldt,Maria J. Molina,Alexander Kerr,Adam Yang,Thomas R. H. Holmes,Christopher R. Hain*

Main category: physics.ao-ph

TL;DR: The paper introduces 'Urban Heat MiniCubes,' an analysis-ready public dataset for studying urban heat, formatted for machine learning and designed to minimize preprocessing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Quantifying street-level urban heat variability is challenging due to the lack of consistent, analysis-ready datasets from multiple sensors at necessary scales.

Method: The authors created 'Urban Heat MiniCubes,' harmonized gridded data covering 48 cities, integrating satellite observations from Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-1, GOES-R, and a microwave land surface temperature product.

Result: Harmonized datasets with reprojected and collocated variables were formed, enabling consistent urban heat analysis with minimized preprocessing efforts and documented technical assessments.

Conclusion: Urban Heat MiniCubes democratizes access to street-level urban heat data for machine learning, provides FAIR data for multi-sensor applications, and enables broader and more efficient urban heat research.

Abstract: Urban heat is amplified by impermeable surfaces and heterogeneous built environments, yet street-level variability remains difficult to quantify because multi-sensor observations are rarely available in consistent, analysis-ready form at the necessary spatiotemporal scales. We present "Urban Heat MiniCubes," a publicly available, FAIR-oriented dataset designed for machine learning applications in urban heat research. The dataset provides harmonized 90 x 90 km gridded data cubes for 48 cities in the Western Hemisphere spanning 2022-2023, with variables reprojected and collocated to a common grid to reduce preprocessing (e.g., reprojection, resampling, and spatiotemporal alignment). Urban Heat MiniCubes includes two complementary modalities: (i) higher-spatial-resolution, lower-frequency observations from Landsat 8/9 (e.g., surface reflectances) and Sentinel-1 (e.g., synthetic aperture radar backscatter), and (ii) higher-temporal-frequency, coarser observations from GOES-R (e.g., longwave infrared brightness temperatures) and a microwave land surface temperature product. We document variables and metadata and provide technical assessment using inter-variable analyses and autoencoder-based reconstruction-error summaries across pixel classes (e.g., water and cloud). Potential use cases and limitations are also discussed.

</details>


<div id='astro-ph.IM'></div>

# astro-ph.IM [[Back]](#toc)

### [472] [Time Series Analysis in Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11746)
*Antonio Pagliaro,Anna Anzalone*

Main category: astro-ph.IM

TL;DR: This chapter reviews time series analysis techniques from both classical and modern machine learning perspectives, with practical examples from various domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide readers with theoretical and practical knowledge to apply machine learning techniques for time series analysis.

Method: The chapter explains traditional time series concepts, classical statistical models like ARIMA, and modern machine learning tools such as RNNs, CNNs, and transformers.

Result: The chapter identifies common principles across applications in astronomy, weather forecasting, and finance, illustrating how methods can be applied.

Conclusion: Readers gain a comprehensive understanding of time series techniques, empowering them to apply machine learning methods effectively in diverse fields.

Abstract: Time series analysis is a fundamental component of machine learning, especially in astrophysics and cosmology where temporal data abound. This chapter provides a pedagogical review of time series analysis techniques from a machine learning perspective. We cover the basic concepts of time series (stationarity, autocorrelation, seasonality), classical statistical models (autoregressive, moving average, ARIMA, exponential smoothing, state-space models), and modern machine learning approaches. In particular, we discuss how traditional statistical methods lay the groundwork, and then explore machine learning methods for time series, including feature-based regression, tree-based ensemble methods, hidden Markov models, Gaussian processes, and deep learning models (recurrent neural networks, convolutional networks, transformers). Throughout, we illustrate with examples drawn from multiple domains (e.g. astronomy, weather forecasting, finance) to emphasize common principles. The goal is to equip readers with both the theoretical understanding and practical context to apply machine learning techniques for time series analysis in their research.

</details>


<div id='math.NA'></div>

# math.NA [[Back]](#toc)

### [473] [Adjoint Method versus Physics-Informed Neural Networks in PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12337)
*Zhen Zhang,Alessandro Alla,George Em Karniadakis*

Main category: math.NA

TL;DR: The paper compares adjoint-based optimization and physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) for solving inverse problems governed by PDEs, finding that the best method depends on the representation of the unknown variables.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To fairly compare the performance of adjoint-based optimization and PINNs for solving PDE-constrained inverse problems, as prior comparisons varied in formulations and setups.

Method: Both methods were instantiated using identical setups, such as equations, domains, and regularization terms, while ensuring consistent parameterizations and optimizers. Benchmarks included various PDE problems like Burgers, Darcy permeability, Allen-Cahn, and Navier-Stokes equations.

Result: The choice between adjoint-based optimization and PINNs depends on the representation of the unknown parameter. For grid-based fields, adjoint methods are favored, while PINNs are better suited for neural representations. Time-dependent problems also showed PINNs as cost-effective; using PINNs to warm-start adjoint strategies achieved accuracy with reduced cost.

Conclusion: Each method has strengths that make it more suitable for specific setups, and a hybrid approach leveraging PINNs and adjoint optimization can combine their advantages effectively.

Abstract: Inverse problems governed by partial differential equations (PDEs) are central to computational mechanics and are commonly solved by adjoint-based optimization, while physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a flexible alternative. Their relative performance remains difficult to assess because the two approaches are often compared under different formulations, parameterizations, optimizers, and regularization choices. We present a fair comparison of adjoint optimization and PINNs for PDE-constrained inverse problems. From a common abstract formulation, we instantiate both methods on identical domains, governing equations, observation models, and regularization terms, while matching the optimizer, unknown parameterization, and arithmetic precision wherever applicable. The benchmarks include unsteady Burgers, noisy Darcy permeability inversion, three-dimensional Allen--Cahn reaction identification, and unsteady Navier--Stokes viscosity identification. The results show that the representation of the unknown largely determines the preferred method: grid-based fields favor the discrete adjoint, whereas neural representations are native to PINNs and relevant for closure and constitutive modeling. For time-dependent problems, adjoint inversion can be dominated by trajectory storage and differentiation, while PINNs provide satisfactory reconstructions at lower cost. A PINN-warm-started adjoint strategy then recovers adjoint-level accuracy at substantially reduced cost.

</details>


<div id='econ.TH'></div>

# econ.TH [[Back]](#toc)

### [474] [Market Design for AI: Beyond the Copyright Binary](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12260)
*Yan Dai,Maryam Farboodi,Negin Golrezaei,Sepehr Shahshahani*

Main category: econ.TH

TL;DR: This paper addresses the challenges of designing a fair market for human-generated content, balancing incentives for creators and the advancement of AI training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the shortcomings in current approaches to using human-generated content for AI training, notably the lack of creator compensation and the inherent flaws in intellectual property rights-based models.

Method: Analyzed free-for-all and intellectual property rights models using a static Stackelberg game, and extended it to a dynamic framework highlighting market inefficiencies like the "originality penalty" and "curse of precision." Proposed a new model involving a data intermediary.

Result: The proposed market design mitigates externalities and restores efficiency by subsidizing innovative contributions through a data intermediary.

Conclusion: Both current extremes (free-for-all and strong property rights) are flawed; the proposed solution promotes fairness for creators and preserves AI performance through a better market design.

Abstract: How can we design a market of human-generated content for use in training AI models that both enables technological progress and preserves individual incentives for high-quality content creation? Existing approaches take polar positions: a "free-for-all" model based on fair use and a "strong intellectual property rights" model. We show that both fail: Free-for-all does not compensate creators, and -- by modeling as a static Stackelberg game -- strong intellectual property rights also underpower creative incentives. We find this especially true for more innovative creators, a phenomenon we term the "originality penalty." Extending this insight to a dynamic model, we find another market failure undermining AI model performance, even for an initially good model: Such a model induces greater reliance by humans on AI-assisted creation, resulting in homogenized content feeding back into training, which degrades the model performance -- a "curse of precision." We further propose a market design with a data intermediary internalizing cross-creator externalities and subsidizing innovative contributions, thereby restoring efficiency.

</details>


<div id='cs.IR'></div>

# cs.IR [[Back]](#toc)

### [475] [A PubMed-Scale Dataset of Structured Biomedical Abstracts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11361)
*Chia-Hsuan Chang,Haerin Song,Brian Ondov,Hua Xu*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper introduces Structured PubMed, a dataset compiling over 23.2 million structured biomedical abstracts, enabling improved text-mining processes.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Unstructured abstracts in PubMed hinder biomedical text processing and limit information retrieval and mining.

Method: Compiled a dataset of 5.9 million author-structured abstracts and 17.2 million machine-structured abstracts, harmonized under a five-section schema using a Large Language Model pipeline.

Result: Created a unified and extensive biomedical abstract dataset mapped to metadata like PubMed IDs and publication details.

Conclusion: Structured PubMed enables better training, benchmarking, and large-scale information extraction from biomedical texts.

Abstract: Structured abstracts are important for biomedical literature processing, by facilitating information retrieval, text mining, and knowledge synthesis. However, a vast portion of abstracts indexed in PubMed remain unstructured, presenting a significant bottleneck for downstream text-processing workflows and applications. To resolve this limitation, we introduce Structured PubMed, a comprehensive corpus of section-labeled biomedical abstracts compiled from the complete PubMed database, encompassing over 23.2 million research-article records. The corpus is divided into two distinct subsets: a collection of 5.9 million author-structured abstracts parsed from official XML files, and an automatically labeled collection of 17.2 million originally unstructured abstracts structured via a verbatim-extraction Large Language Model pipeline. Every record is harmonized under a unified five-section schema and mapped to its original PubMed identifier, publication type, and publication date. This dataset can be utilized to train sentence-classification models, benchmark text-segmentation architectures, and perform large-scale, section-specific information extraction at an unprecedented PubMed-wide scale.

</details>


### [476] [Factions Within, Uncertain Across: Within-Document Reader Sub-Groups in Social Highlighting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11613)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper explores whether groups of readers highlight text in stable factions both within and across documents. It finds clear factions within a document but cannot determine stability across documents.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand if collective highlighting behavior by readers forms distinct sub-groups and whether these factions are stable across documents or are context-dependent.

Method: The authors conducted two experiments: (1) analyzing within-document reader sub-groups using a margin-preserving curveball null and region-preserving null, and (2) testing the cross-document stability of reader agreements through split-half reproducibility in a co-readership context.

Result: Experiment 1 identified strong sub-groups within documents, with agreement significantly exceeding chance levels. Shared regional engagement explained 40% of agreement, leaving finer reader-specific agreements significant. Experiment 2 failed to conclude whether these factions are stable across documents due to low power and imprecise results.

Conclusion: The study concludes that within-document factions exist among readers, indicating that agreement is more granular and reader-specific than previously thought. However, the stability of these factions across different documents remains unresolved due to insufficient data.

Abstract: When many people highlight the same document, is the crowd a single consensus, or is it internally structured into reader sub-groups that mark different things -- and is that structure a stable property of a reader or of the document? Building on prior work showing an individual's within-document highlighting signal is a whisper while individuality lives in selection, we ask the group-level question on a co-readership platform using a margin-preserving curveball null. Experiment 1: within a document, readers form strong sub-groups -- pairs agree far beyond what shared salience, mark density, and sentence popularity predict (nearest-neighbour agreement z=+6.3, significant in 88% of documents). Under an eight-block region-preserving null, shared engagement with the same coarse regions of the document accounts for about 40% of this excess; the majority survives as finer reader-specific agreement (z=+3.6, 77% significant). So the within-document crowd is, in a descriptive sense, factional. Experiment 2: is that grouping a stable reader trait? Here we are honest about power. The cross-document split-half reproducibility of a pair's agreement is near zero pooled (+0.078 and 0.000 in two separately drawn samples), and a power calibration shows the test is informative only for pairs that co-read many documents. In the only informative high-overlap subset (k>=4), point estimates are positive but small-sample, imprecise across the separately drawn samples, never significant, and attenuate under the region-preserving null. We therefore leave cross-document stability unresolved: the data is consistent with anything from situational grouping to a weak-to-moderate stable reader trait. The crowd is factional within a document; whether its factions follow the reader across documents is, honestly, beyond our reach.

</details>


### [477] [The Long Tail, Not the Front Page: Cold-Start Prediction of Crowd Highlight Salience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11654)
*Kazuki Nakayashiki,Keisuke Watanabe*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The study evaluates whether crowd-sourced document highlight predictions can outperform a lead sentence baseline using a trained logistic ranker. The model yields a small but significant improvement over unsupervised methods and baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate if aggregate crowd salience of document highlights can be predicted from textual features without waiting for marks to accumulate, and to determine if trained models can outperform trivial baselines.

Method: The researchers used a logistic ranker over sentence embeddings combined with positional/contextual features, compared against trivial and unsupervised baselines. They analyzed performance differences with a statistical approach, including bootstrap resampling, ablation studies, and regression analyses.

Result: The trained model marginally outperformed the lead baseline by +0.044 average precision and significantly beat unsupervised baselines. It also maintained precision@3 improvement (+55%) and outperformed the baseline on 69% of documents.

Conclusion: Predicting highlight signals from text alone is feasible with a trained model. Gains are driven by reader mark learning and depend on popularity and label reliability, with reduced gains for highly popular content.

Abstract: A social highlighter's most useful signal -- which passages a crowd of readers marks -- exists only for documents people have already read. Can the aggregate crowd salience of a document be predicted from its text before its marks accumulate? Prior work on this data found that zero-shot language models recover highlight locations worse than a trivial lead (position) baseline, so we ask whether a model trained on the highlight corpus can beat that baseline. Using a pre-registered ladder of models and a by-document cluster bootstrap, we find a small but robust edge: a logistic ranker over sentence embeddings and positional/contextual features beats the lead baseline by +0.044 average precision (95% CI [+0.029, +0.058]; clears a pre-registered margin delta=0.03 in 97% of resamples, and stable across pipeline re-runs). Two unsupervised extractive baselines (centroid, LexRank-style centrality) lose to lead, and the trained model beats them by +0.108, so the edge is not recovered by generic unsupervised proxies -- it reflects learning from real reader marks. In product terms, precision@3 rises from 0.25 to 0.39 (+55% relative) and the model beats lead on 69% of documents. An ablation attributes the edge to the raw embedding (+0.014) and training augmentation (+0.010), each with a positive CI. The edge is not a temporal-generalization failure, and we find no evidence that content drift or near-duplicate leakage explains it. A standardized regression shows the advantage is governed mainly by document popularity (lower popularity, larger edge) and by label reliability. It nearly vanishes only on the most popular content; there it is the lead baseline that strengthens, not the model that weakens. Because our evaluation conditions on documents that eventually accumulated readers, these results are a retrospective cold-start simulation.

</details>


### [478] [What Limits Does Quantization Place on Dense Top-$k$ Retrieval? A Theoretical Study](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11780)
*Koki Okajima,Tsukasa Yoshida*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: The paper investigates embedding conditions for documents in vector databases, establishing bounds and dependencies on dimension and precision for achieving reliable top-k retrieval. It highlights that real-world systems require scaling embedding properties with corpus size.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand and formalize the relationships between embedding dimensions, precision, and corpus size for reliable top-k retrieval, particularly to address gaps in corpus-independent assumptions for finite precision systems.

Method: Theoretical analysis of embedding conditions, examining infinite precision embeddings and realistic finite precision scenarios. Derived thresholds and bounds on embedding dimensions and precision necessary for guaranteed top-k retrieval, including specific quantization models.

Result: The study demonstrates that while infinite precision allows for corpus-independent bounds, finite precision imposes corpus-dependent constraints. Specifically, embedding dimension (Bd) must grow at least logarithmically with corpus size (N), and no embedding is feasible below a precision threshold (B=O(ln ln N)).

Conclusion: Practical retrieval systems with quantized embeddings need to scale dimension and/or precision with corpus size to maintain top-k retrieval performance, contrasting with idealized infinite precision results.

Abstract: We establish conditions for embedding a corpus of $N$ documents as $d$-dimensional vectors such that every $k$-subset $S \subseteq [N]$ is realizable as a result of top-$k$ retrieval by some query vector. Recent work shows that $d = O(k)$ suffices for such embeddings to exist in $\mathbb{R}^d$, independently of $N$. We theoretically prove that this corpus-independent bound is specific to infinite precision. With $B$ bits per coordinate, perfect top-$k$ retrieval requires $Bd = Ω(k \ln N)$; thus, at any fixed precision, the dimension must grow at least logarithmically with $N$. Specializing to a $\ell_2$-normalized $B$-bit uniform scalar quantization model, we also identify a threshold on the precision $B^{*} = O(\ln \ln N)$ below which no dimension suffices, together with two further regimes that bound the feasible $(B, d)$ pairs. Our result implies that in practical vector databases and dense retrieval systems where quantization is standard, the embedding dimension and possibly the precision must grow with the corpus size.

</details>


### [479] [DiffCold: A Diffusion-based Generative Model for Cold-Start Item Recommendation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12245)
*Kangning Zhang,Yingjie Qin,Weinan Zhang,Yong Yu,Jianghao Lin*

Main category: cs.IR

TL;DR: DiffCold, a diffusion-based generative model, tackles the challenge of cold-start item recommendation by bridging the gap between warm and cold item representations with tailored mechanisms, outperforming existing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper seeks to solve the seesaw dilemma in cold-start item recommendation, where improving performance for cold items compromises warm items and vice versa, caused by distributional disparity in their embeddings.

Method: The authors propose DiffCold, a diffusion-based generative model leveraging conditional diffusion for embedding reconstruction, paired with a Retrieval-enhanced Aggregator and a Simulation-based Representation Alignment module.

Result: DiffCold resolves the seesaw dilemma and outperforms state-of-the-art methods on three benchmarks by maintaining representation consistency between warm and cold items.

Conclusion: DiffCold successfully unifies warm and cold item embeddings, preserving their respective manifold structures and ensuring robust performance for cold-start recommendation tasks.

Abstract: Cold-start item recommendation remains a persistent challenge in real-world systems due to the absence of interaction histories. While prior models attempt to bridge this gap using item content features, they universally suffer from the \textbf{seesaw dilemma}: enhancing performance for cold items inevitably degrades performance for warm items, and vice versa. We identify that this dilemma stems from a fundamental \textbf{distributional disparity}: warm item embeddings occupy a complex ``behavioral manifold" shaped by rich interaction signals, whereas cold item embeddings are constrained to a ``semantic manifold" derived solely from auxiliary content. Existing methods often force a rigid mapping between these inconsistent spaces, causing the model to sacrifice the precision of warm representations to accommodate cold ones. To address this, we propose \textbf{DiffCold}, a diffusion-based generative model that unifies warm and cold representations. Unlike GANs or VAEs, DiffCold leverages conditional diffusion to reconstruct warm item embeddings from content, preserving the underlying manifold structure without degradation. We further tailor this paradigm with two specific designs: a \textbf{Retrieval-enhanced Aggregator} that initializes generation using semantically similar warm items to bypass inefficient noise, and a \textbf{Simulation-based Representation Alignment} module that enforces distribution consistency between generated and real embeddings via contrastive learning. Experiments on three benchmarks confirm that DiffCold resolves the seesaw dilemma, consistently outperforming state-of-the-art methods across all metrics.

</details>


<div id='cs.DB'></div>

# cs.DB [[Back]](#toc)

### [480] [LLMs+Graphs: Toward Graph-Native, Synergistic AI Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11560)
*Arijit Khan,Longxu Sun,Xin Huang*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper explores the integration of large language models (LLMs) with graph computation and algorithms, highlighting novel synergies and their implications for advanced AI systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of LLMs in structured and multi-hop reasoning, and to explore their potential when integrated with graph computation for real-world, data-rich applications.

Method: The authors propose three synergies between LLMs and graph computation: LLM-augmented graph reasoning, dual integration of LLMs with knowledge graphs for mutual enhancement, and the use of graph algorithms in advanced AI workflows. They also present LLM-based advancements in graph data management and hybrid models with graph neural networks.

Result: The paper synthesizes the methods and design principles that integrate LLMs with graph-based data and computation, providing a unified framework for researchers in data science and AI.

Conclusion: Integrating LLMs with graph computation can lead to powerful AI systems capable of richer, context-driven reasoning and decision-making, with significant implications for both theory and applications in various domains.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have advanced rapidly, but their limitations in structured and multi-hop reasoning underscore the need for graph-native, synergistic artificial intelligence (AI) systems. Graph-structured data underpins critical applications across social, biological, financial, transportation, web, and knowledge domains, making it essential to understand how LLMs can leverage graph computation for grounded, context-rich inference. Three complementary synergies are emerging: LLMs augmented with graph computation for retrieval and reasoning; bidirectional integration between LLMs and knowledge graphs (KGs), where LLMs support KG construction and curation while KGs enforce semantic constraints and factual consistency; and AI agents strengthened by graph algorithms for planning, decision making, and multi-step reasoning. In parallel, LLMs introduce new capabilities for graph data management and graph machine learning (ML) through natural language interfaces and hybrid LLM-graph neural network (GNN) pipelines. This tutorial synthesizes the algorithms, systems, and design principles driving these converging directions, offering data science and data mining researchers a unified perspective on integrating LLMs, graph data management, graph mining, graph ML, and agentic computation into next-generation graph-native AI systems.

</details>


### [481] [Neuro-Relational Programs: Unifying Queries and Neural Computation over Structured Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11946)
*Arie Soeteman,Balder ten Cate,Maurice Funk,Benny Kimelfeld,Carsten Lutz,Moritz Schönherr*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: Neuro-Relational Programs (NRPs) are introduced as a declarative query language combining neural computation with relational reasoning, extending traditional database query approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current techniques for neural networks with relational databases either focus on graph representations or direct tuple embeddings. The authors aim to unify these approaches in a formal, expressive framework.

Method: The authors integrate Datalog-style rules with trainable neural computation components, enabling both querying and learning in relational databases simultaneously.

Result: NRPs unify existing architectures like GNNs and Deep Homomorphism Networks and are characterized by their expressive power, connecting them to logic and computational complexity frameworks.

Conclusion: NRPs offer a unified, expressive approach for integrating neural computation and database querying, bridging the gap between logical reasoning and machine learning.

Abstract: The conventional approach to deep learning over relational databases applies neural models, such as Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), to a graph representation of the database. Recent approaches instead operate on databases directly, associating tuples with embeddings and extending query mechanisms to jointly process embeddings and relational content. Inspired by these developments, we introduce Neuro-Relational Programs (NRPs), a declarative query language for relational databases whose facts carry numeric vector embeddings. NRPs extend Datalog-style rules with operations that combine, aggregate, and transform embeddings, thereby interleaving relational reasoning and learnable neural components within a single formalism. This yields a general approach to neural computation over relational data: an NRP can be read both as a query plan with trainable components and as a neural architecture with relational structure built in.
  Natural syntactic fragments of NRPs recover existing architectures and query formalisms. Zero-ary NRPs correspond to non-adaptive query algorithms; monadic NRPs generalize GNN-style message passing and precisely capture Deep Homomorphism Networks, a connection that we extend to frontier-guarded NRPs over databases with row-ids. We characterize the expressive power of unrestricted NRPs with ReLU-FFN transformations by FOCQ, an extension of first-order logic with counting interpreted over real-weighted structures, yielding a precise connection with uniform TC$^0$ over ordered databases. Together, these results establish NRPs as a broad declarative framework for querying and neural computation over relational data.

</details>


### [482] [TAHOE: Text-to-SQL with Automated Hint Optimization from Experience](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12387)
*Zhiyi Chen,Jie Song,Peng Li*

Main category: cs.DB

TL;DR: The paper introduces Tahoe, a system to optimize Text-to-SQL performance for Large Language Models by leveraging a dynamic Hint Bank for error correction and dialect adaptation, achieving significant improvements over traditional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in deploying Text-to-SQL systems in real-world settings, such as handling varying SQL dialects, large schemas, and evolving user preferences, without relying on costly supervised fine-tuning or inefficient test-time scaling.

Method: Tahoe employs a hint-learning pipeline to consolidate debugging feedback into a structured "Hint Bank". It generates Syntax Hints for dialect-specific rules, Semantic Hints for schema- and user-specific logic, and utilizes a Strategy Layer to reconcile conflicting user intents. At inference, Tahoe retrieves relevant hints to guide LLMs through logic planning and SQL synthesis.

Result: Tahoe significantly improves Text-to-SQL performance. For example, on Spider 2.0-Snow using GPT-5.5, it raises pass rates from 61.95% to 79.42%, and improves pass-at-4 from 72.57% to 87.61%. It also achieves 100% syntax pass rates for Snowflake and reduces compiler-feedback iterations drastically.

Conclusion: Tahoe demonstrates that prompt optimization using a structured pipeline and Hint Bank can address deployment challenges and improve SQL query generation without fine-tuning model parameters, proving its broad applicability to different models.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have democratized database access through Text-to-SQL, but moving from prototypes to production remains difficult. Real deployments must handle strict SQL dialects, massive schemas, and evolving user preferences, while supervised fine-tuning is costly and rigid and agentic test-time scaling is expensive. We present Tahoe, a system that treats prompt optimization as a dynamic data management problem. Tahoe uses an error-driven hint learning pipeline across Development and Deployment to consolidate debugging traces into a structured Hint Bank. Compiler feedback is distilled into reusable Syntax Hints for dialect-specific rules, while execution and user feedback are converted into Semantic Hints for schema- and user-specific logic. Tahoe further introduces a Strategy Layer that models conflicting user intents as competing strategies under shared natural-language triggers, with recency signals and post-learning attribution statistics that summarize empirical success, harm, inertness, and support. At inference time, Tahoe retrieves relevant hints and guides the LLM through Logic Planning followed by SQL Synthesis. We implement and evaluate the development-phase workflow, leaving deployment-time human-feedback updates for future work. On Spider 2.0-Snow, Tahoe substantially improves Text-to-SQL without updating model parameters. On 113 supervised Spider 2.0-Snow-0212 examples using GPT-5.5, Tahoe raises pass rate from 61.95 percent to 79.42 percent and pass-at-4 from 72.57 percent to 87.61 percent, achieves 100 percent Snowflake syntax pass rate, and reduces average compiler-feedback critic rounds from 2.79 to 0.12 per sampled candidate. The same Hint Bank also transfers to weaker backbones, including a 19.7 percentage-point pass-rate gain on Doubao-2.0-lite.

</details>


<div id='cs.HC'></div>

# cs.HC [[Back]](#toc)

### [483] [3-Key-Input: Exploring the Theoretical Minimum Keys for Text Entry](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11642)
*Naoki Kimura*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores how few physical keys an ambiguous keyboard can have, while remaining functional, when paired with modern language models for accurate text input.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To increase flexibility in hardware design for constrained environments, like assistive devices or small mobile form factors, by reducing the number of physical keyboard keys needed.

Method: Systematic evaluation of text entry systems with 2-5 physical keys combined with language-model-based disambiguation. The study compares 3 types of letter-to-key mappings and 3 decoding techniques on a diverse English corpus.

Result: The best performance is achieved with 3 keys and GPT-4o, achieving a 59% reduction in error rates compared to 2-key systems. Mapping choice minimally affects performance, but technical content increases error rates compared to business text.

Conclusion: Three physical keys are an effective minimum for English text input under a strong language model, balancing performance and hardware design constraints for assistive or compact devices.

Abstract: How far can we reduce the number of physical keys if we endow an ambiguous keyboard with modern language models? Fewer keys increase hardware design freedom in constrained settings such as assistive devices and mobile form factors. This paper systematically evaluates text entry systems using 2-5 physical keys combined with language-model-based disambiguation. On a 300-sentence English corpus (100 sentences each for Business / Conversational / Technical), we compare key counts (2-5), letter-to-key mappings (layout-based / frequency-based / intentionally worst-case), and decoders (Trie-only, GPT-2 beam search, GPT-4o selection). We find that 3 keys + GPT-4o achieves character error rate (CER) 9.46% and word error rate (WER) 12.20%, reducing CER by 59% relative to 2 keys (CER 23.3%). At 3 keys, the key-stream entropy is 1.54 bits/char; while increasing to 5 keys improves accuracy (CER 5.4%), the marginal gains diminish. Mapping choice has a small impact under standard designs (ΔCER < 0.5 pp), and even an intentionally worst mapping degrades CER by only +0.5 pp, whereas Technical sentences yield roughly twice the error rate of Business. These results suggest that, in our evaluated offline setting under a strong LM prior, 3 keys are a practical minimum for general English.

</details>


### [484] [Frozen Multimodal Embeddings for Personality and Cognitive Ability Assessment in Asynchronous Video Interviews](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11930)
*Kuo-En Hung,Hung-Yue Suen,Shih-Ching Yeh,Hsiang-Wen Wang*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper explores methods for predicting psychological traits from asynchronous video interviews by leveraging trait-specific multimodal modeling, achieving notable performance improvements in personality and cognitive ability prediction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The motivation is to address challenges in multimodal learning for psychological assessments using AVIs, considering limited annotated datasets and the high-dimensional nature of data.

Method: The study uses frozen multimodal encoders (e.g., CLIP, Whisper, RoBERTa) without fine-tuning and applies low-capacity downstream models. It also implements trait-specific regression and late fusion strategies for improved performance.

Result: For Track 1 (HEXACO traits prediction), their method achieved a significant improvement in average MSE (0.2696 vs. 0.3334 baseline). For Track 2 (cognitive ability classification), they achieved above-baseline accuracy but identified dataset shortcuts as a limitation.

Conclusion: AVI-based psychological assessments benefit from trait-specific multimodal approaches. However, ensuring robustness in cognitive ability prediction requires better handling of dataset-related shortcuts.

Abstract: Predicting psychological traits from asynchronous video interviews (AVIs) is a challenging multimodal learning problem because labeled datasets are limited while each response contains high-dimensional visual, acoustic, and verbal signals. This paper presents our solution for the ACM Multimedia AVI Challenge 2026, which evaluates two tasks: Track~1 predicts self-reported HEXACO personality traits from personality-related interview responses, and Track~2 classifies cognitive ability levels from structured AVI responses. We treat the problem as a small-sample representation learning task. Instead of fine-tuning large pretrained models, we use frozen multimodal encoders, including CLIP for visual features, Whisper for acoustic features and transcripts, and RoBERTa, E5, and DeBERTaV3 for textual representations, followed by low-capacity downstream models. For Track~1, our trait-specific regression and late-fusion system achieves an average validation MSE of 0.2696, improving over the official baseline of 0.3334. Ablation results show a three-step improvement from a global model (0.3189), to per-trait modeling (0.2871), to per-trait late fusion (0.2696), corresponding to a 19.1\% relative MSE reduction over the official baseline. For Track~2, a compact subject-attribute baseline reaches 0.5781 accuracy, while our multimodal ensemble reaches 0.5313, both above the official baseline of 0.4062. We interpret this result as evidence of possible subject-attribute shortcuts in the validation split rather than robust cognitive inference from AVI content. Overall, our findings suggest that AVI-based psychological assessment benefits from trait-specific multimodal modeling, but cognitive ability prediction requires careful control of dataset shortcuts.

</details>


### [485] [Designing AI-Supported Focus Groups: A Role x Modality Playbook](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11835)
*Zhiqing Wang,Steven Dow*

Main category: cs.HC

TL;DR: This paper examines how generative AI can support focus groups in design research, proposing an AI-focused playbook to address challenges like facilitation and engagement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper is motivated by the need to address the resource-intensive nature of focus groups and their reliance on skilled moderation, while exploring how AI can enhance these processes.

Method: The authors synthesize prior work on AI-supported live conversations and translate these insights into a detailed playbook tailored to focus groups, categorizing AI roles and modalities.

Result: The paper offers an organized framework differentiating AI roles (tool, co-host, host) and modalities (text, voice, embodied) to guide the use of AI in focus group facilitation.

Conclusion: The study highlights interactional trade-offs and frames open questions to assess the methodological implications of AI in focus groups, thereby offering a roadmap for future exploration.

Abstract: Collecting participants' lived experiences is central to design research. Focus groups are uniquely valuable because participants not only share individual accounts but also respond to one another, surfacing comparison, disagreement, and collective sensemaking. However, focus groups are resource-intensive and highly sensitive to facilitation: moderators must probe for specificity, balance participation, manage topic flow, and sustain psychological safety, and subtle facilitation choices can shape what becomes salient. Recent HCI work and commercial meeting tools show that generative AI can scaffold live conversation through prompting, turn regulation, thematic mapping, and real-time summarization. Yet UXR teams lack a clear map of what these capabilities mean in focus groups and what methodological risks they introduce. We synthesize AI supports for live conversation and translate them into a focus-group-specific playbook organized by AI role (tool, co-host, host) and modality (text, voice, embodied).We synthesize prior work on AI-supported live conversation and propose a focus-group-specific playbook of AI supports organized by role (tool, co-host, host) and modality (text, voice, embodied). We characterize interactional trade-offs and identify open questions for evaluating AI-supported focus groups as methodological configurations.

</details>


<div id='cs.CE'></div>

# cs.CE [[Back]](#toc)

### [486] [Neural-Parameterized Cellular Automata for Wildfire Spread](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11676)
*Maksym Zhenirovskyy,Ion Matei,Rohit Vuppala,Takuya Kurihana,Hon Yung Wonga*

Main category: cs.CE

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid deep-learning technique for more accurate wildfire forecasting using Probabilistic Cellular Automata and neural networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current wildfire models underpredict fire spread due to reliance on static parameters and maps, necessitating a more adaptive and dynamic modeling approach.

Method: The paper introduces a hybrid framework combining cellular automata with multi-scale convolutional neural networks to model fire dynamics. Hardware acceleration and parameter calibration are achieved through JAX.

Result: The model achieves an IoU > 0.6 over 72-hour forecast horizons in six large-scale wildfires, demonstrating high accuracy through incremental fitting using observed data.

Conclusion: The proposed method integrates interpretability and dynamic adaptability, offering a viable solution for improved wildfire spread forecasting.

Abstract: Traditional wildfire models rely on rigid, low-dimensional parameters and static fuel maps, frequently underpredicting fire spread. To address this weakness, we introduce a hybrid deep-learning parameterized Probabilistic Cellular Automata (CA) framework implemented in JAX. Our approach employs a Multi-Scale Convolutional Neural Network to dynamically generate spatially varying parameters that govern fire-spread probability, wind alignment, and slope influence. This hybrid design captures complex, nonlinear environmental interactions while preserving the physical interpretability of the underlying three-state CA. The JAX implementation enables hardware acceleration and gradient-based parameter calibration. Evaluated on six large-scale wildfires in the western United States, the model maintains IoU > 0.6 over 72-hour forecast horizons after a 10-day data assimilation window during which the model is fitted incrementally to observed perimeters; the resulting forecast is a conditional projection of fire growth under the suppression regime already ncoded in those observations.

</details>


<div id='physics.comp-ph'></div>

# physics.comp-ph [[Back]](#toc)

### [487] [Physically Constrained Ensemble Gaussian Process Modelling for Expensive Quantum Systems with Heteroskedastic Noise](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11240)
*Arpan Biswas,Surtirtha Paul,Joseph Agada,Matthias Thamm,Adrian Del Maestro*

Main category: physics.comp-ph

TL;DR: The study introduces a Physically Constrained Ensemble Gaussian Process (pc-EGP) framework to model complex and noisy quantum systems while enforcing physical consistency constraints, achieving improved accuracy and meaningful predictions compared to conventional methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods like DMRG and QMC are computationally expensive and limited in parameter exploration, with variable errors that require accurate quantification and propagation.

Method: The framework integrates physical constraints into the Gaussian Process (GP) loss function and uses an ensemble of GP models trained with noisy data through numerical quadrature, forming a weighted average.

Result: In case studies involving synthetic data, DMRG Bose-Hubbard Model simulations, and QMC simulations of quantum liquids, pc-EGP demonstrated superior performance in balancing accuracy and physical relevance.

Conclusion: pc-EGP provides an efficient way to model complex quantum systems with heteroskedastic noise while maintaining physical constraints, offering improved predictive accuracy and meaningful estimations of quantum parameters.

Abstract: Accurate modeling of quantum many-body systems often requires computationally expensive simulations such as Density Matrix Renormalization Group (DMRG) or Quantum Monte Carlo (QMC) calculations. These methods, while precise, impose significant time and resource constraints, limiting their use in exhaustive parameter exploration. Moreover, these expensive simulations can contain variable errors over the large unknown parameter space, which needs to be quantified and propagated. Thus, predictive modelling is required to estimate the functional space accurately over scarcely sampled data with heteroskedastic noise, while preserving the physical relevance of the estimation. Therefore, we present a Physically Constrained Ensemble Gaussian Process (pc-EGP) framework designed to efficiently model complex and noisy quantum systems under physical consistency constraints. The proposed method first enforces physical constraints as a user controlled weighted penalty to the data-driven loss function of the Gaussian Process (GP) surrogates. Then an ensemble of such GP models is trained with variable noisy simulations via numerical quadrature method where these multiple GP(s) at different nodes is integrated as a quadrature weighted average. We first demonstrate the framework on synthetically generated data before applying to quantum systems. In the first case study, we leverage DMRG simulations of the Bose-Hubbard Model to predict the critical interaction parameter Uc governing the superfluid-to-Mott-insulator transition. In the second case study, we demonstrate our method on QMC simulations, of a quantum liquid confined inside a nanoporous silicate with the goal of optimizing a chemical environment to realize a one-dimensional superfluid. Compared to conventional GP, pc-EGP achieves a better balance of accuracy and physically meaningful predictions.

</details>


<div id='cs.CR'></div>

# cs.CR [[Back]](#toc)

### [488] [Partitioned Tags, Shared Data: Reconciling Strict Cache Isolation with Write-Shared Coherence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.12259)
*Kartik Ramkrishnan,Stephen McCamant,Antonia Zhai,Pen Chung Yew*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: SCP (Secure and Coherent Partitioning) resolves a decade-old issue in cache partitioning by ensuring strict eviction isolation with write-shared coherence, mitigating major cache side channel attacks with minimal hardware cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The paper addresses a long-standing issue in secure shared-OS environments where write-shared coherence conflicts with strict cache partitioning, blocking the deployment of effective structural defenses against cache side-channel attacks.

Method: SCP achieves its results by partitioning only the tags while sharing a single data pool sized to prevent cross-partition eviction, adding timing obfuscation, and mitigating coherence-based leakage through specific write-routing strategies.

Result: In simulations using gem5, SCP mitigated major cache side-channel attacks like Prime+Probe and Flush+Reload, yielding results comparable to random guessing. Shared-writeable-line attacks were also neutralized, with only a 2.8% increase in LLC SRAM cost and negligible performance impact.

Conclusion: SCP provides an effective and deployable solution for secure cache partitioning in shared-OS environments, combining strong security guarantees against major side-channel attacks with a minimal hardware impact.

Abstract: Cache partitioning is among the strongest structural defenses against eviction-based cache side channels, yet a decade-old design issue has blocked its widespread deployment in secure shared-OS settings. The issue is that write-shared coherence collapses under strict partitioning. We present SCP (Secure and Coherent Partitioning), which combines strict eviction isolation with write-shared coherence by partitioning only the tags, sharing a single data pool, and sizing the data pool so capacity-driven cross-partition eviction cannot occur. Timing obfuscation extends protections to the inter-partition lookup path. Coherence-based leakage on shared-writeable lines is mitigated by routing those writes through to the LLC once a leakage threshold is crossed, which makes attacker write probe latency independent of victim activity.
  Using gem5 for implementation, SCP mitigates Prime+Probe and Flush+Reload, which are the basis for more sophisticated cache attacks. We also demonstrate that a shared-writeable-line attack is mitigated. All these attacks yield results no better than random guessing. SCP's hardware cost is a modest +2.8% LLC SRAM. Performance matches DAWG within 0.3% IPC on the SPEC CPU2017 benchmarks that we evaluated. Sharing-intensive microbenchmarks demonstrate a tunable security-performance tradeoff based on a system-specified leakage threshold.

</details>


### [489] [Sovereign Assurance Boundary: Certificate-Bound Admission for Agentic Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2606.11632)
*Jun He,Deying Yu*

Main category: cs.CR

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Sovereign Assurance Boundary (SAB), a certificate-based runtime admission layer addressing security gaps in autonomous systems by verifying and controlling high-stakes resource mutations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
